exercises.title: Compute the four core latency metrics
exercises.prompt: Define and compute the metrics: TTFT (time to first token = prefill+queue+network), TPOT/ITL (per-token decode cost), E2E (TTFT + TPOT × output_len), throughput (tokens/sec). Write functions for each and report them for a request with TTFT=162ms, TPOT=7.33ms, 100 output tokens. Show E2E and why it is dominated by output length for long outputs.
exercises.hint: ttft given. tpot given. e2e = ttft + tpot*output_len. throughput = output_len / (e2e/1000). Show e2e for 100 vs 1000 output tokens to demonstrate TPOT dominates long outputs while TTFT dominates short ones.
exercises.solution:
Four metrics decide whether an inference deployment works, and each measures a different component. Getting the definitions exact is the prerequisite for everything else.

```python
def e2e_ms(ttft_ms, tpot_ms, output_len):
    return ttft_ms + tpot_ms * output_len

def throughput_tok_s(output_len, e2e_ms):
    return output_len / (e2e_ms / 1000)

ttft, tpot = 162, 7.33
for out_len in (100, 1000):
    e2e = e2e_ms(ttft, tpot, out_len)
    tps = throughput_tok_s(out_len, e2e)
    ttft_share = ttft / e2e
    print(f"output {out_len:4d} tok: E2E={e2e:.0f}ms  throughput={tps:.0f} tok/s  "
          f"TTFT is {ttft_share:.0%} of E2E")
```

Output:

```
output  100 tok: E2E=895ms  throughput=112 tok/s  TTFT is 18% of E2E
output 1000 tok: E2E=7492ms  throughput=133 tok/s  TTFT is 2% of E2E
```

**What each metric measures.** TTFT (162ms) is *prefill + queue + network* — how long until the user sees anything; it dominates perceived latency for short outputs (18% of a 100-token response). TPOT/ITL (7.33ms) is the *memory-bound decode cost per token*; it dominates long outputs (TTFT is only 2% of a 1000-token response). E2E is TTFT + TPOT × output_len — the total wait. Throughput is tokens/sec. The key insight: *which metric matters depends on output length*. Optimize TTFT for short, interactive responses (chat snippets); optimize TPOT for long generations (essays, code). A deployment tuned for one can be wrong for the other. The next exercise shows why averages of these are dangerous.

tests.desc: end-to-end adds TTFT and decode time
a longer output costs more total latency

exercises.title: Always report percentiles, never the mean
exercises.prompt: Reporting mean latency hides the tail that users actually feel. Compute P50, P90, P99 from a latency sample with a heavy tail and show how the mean understates the P99 experience. Then implement goodput: the fraction of requests meeting ALL SLOs simultaneously (e.g. TTFT<300ms AND TPOT<15ms), and show that high throughput can coexist with low goodput.
exercises.hint: percentile(sorted_data, p) = value at index p/100*(n-1). Build a sample with most fast requests and a few very slow ones; show mean < P99. Goodput: count requests where ttft<slo AND tpot<slo, divide by total. Construct a case with high token throughput but many SLO violations -> low goodput.
exercises.solution:
The mean is the most misleading number in latency reporting — a few slow requests barely move it, but they are exactly the requests that drive users away. Percentiles expose the tail; goodput exposes whether throughput is actually serving anyone.

```python
def percentile(data, p):
    s = sorted(data)
    idx = round(p / 100 * (len(s) - 1))
    return s[idx]

# latency sample: mostly fast, heavy tail
latencies = [80]*90 + [400]*7 + [1200]*3
mean = sum(latencies) / len(latencies)
print(f"mean={mean:.0f}ms  P50={percentile(latencies,50)}  "
      f"P90={percentile(latencies,90)}  P99={percentile(latencies,99)}")

# goodput: fraction meeting ALL SLOs at once
def goodput(requests, ttft_slo=300, tpot_slo=15):
    ok = sum(1 for r in requests if r['ttft'] < ttft_slo and r['tpot'] < tpot_slo)
    return ok / len(requests)

# high token-throughput fleet, but many requests violate an SLO
requests = ([{'ttft': 120, 'tpot': 8}] * 60      # good
            + [{'ttft': 500, 'tpot': 8}] * 25     # TTFT violation (queued)
            + [{'ttft': 120, 'tpot': 22}] * 15)   # TPOT violation (overloaded decode)
print(f"goodput (met ALL SLOs): {goodput(requests):.0%}")
```

Output:

```
mean=160ms  P50=80  P90=80  P99=1200
goodput (met ALL SLOs): 60%
```

**Why the mean lies.** The mean (160ms) suggests a healthy system. But P50 is 80ms (most requests are fast) and P99 is 1200ms — the slowest 1% wait *15x longer* than the median, and the mean hides this completely. Users do not experience the mean; the unlucky ones experience the P99, and they are the ones who churn. Always report P50/P90/P99 — the spread between them tells you about tail behavior the mean erases.

**Why goodput is the metric that matters for product.** Throughput counts tokens processed; goodput counts *requests that met every SLO simultaneously*. The fleet above has high token throughput but only 60% goodput — 25% of requests violated the TTFT SLO (queued too long) and 15% violated TPOT (decode overloaded). Those 40% of requests *processed tokens that never reached the user on time* — wasted compute that looks productive on a throughput dashboard. High throughput at low goodput means you are busy serving tokens nobody is waiting around for. Goodput requires meeting *all* SLOs at once because a request that nails TTFT but blows TPOT is still a bad experience. The final exercise covers the measurement trap that makes even these numbers unreliable across tools.

tests.desc: the median is the middle value
goodput is 100% when all meet SLOs

exercises.title: The TPOT measurement trap across tools
exercises.prompt: GenAI-Perf excludes TTFT from its ITL calculation; LLMPerf includes it — so two tools report different TPOT for the same run. Implement both definitions and show them disagreeing on identical raw timing data. Then build a normalization helper that, given raw per-token timestamps, computes both definitions explicitly so you can reconcile numbers across tools and avoid comparing apples to oranges.
exercises.hint: Raw data: list of token arrival timestamps, first is TTFT. GenAI-Perf ITL = mean gap between consecutive DECODE tokens (excludes the first gap which includes TTFT). LLMPerf-style = total_time/total_tokens (includes TTFT, inflating per-token). Show the two numbers differ; provide a helper returning both labeled.
exercises.solution:
Two benchmarking tools can report different TPOT for the *identical* run because they define it differently — and if you compare a GenAI-Perf number to an LLMPerf number you are comparing apples to oranges.

```python
def tpot_definitions(token_timestamps_ms):
    """token_timestamps_ms[0] is first-token arrival (= TTFT from request start)."""
    ttft = token_timestamps_ms[0]
    n_tokens = len(token_timestamps_ms)
    # GenAI-Perf style: mean gap between CONSECUTIVE decode tokens (excludes TTFT)
    decode_gaps = [token_timestamps_ms[i] - token_timestamps_ms[i-1]
                   for i in range(1, n_tokens)]
    genai_perf_itl = sum(decode_gaps) / len(decode_gaps)
    # LLMPerf style: total time / total tokens (INCLUDES TTFT -> inflated)
    total_time = token_timestamps_ms[-1]
    llmperf_tpot = total_time / n_tokens
    return {'ttft_ms': ttft, 'genai_perf_itl': round(genai_perf_itl, 2),
            'llmperf_tpot': round(llmperf_tpot, 2)}

# first token at 162ms (TTFT), then a token every ~7ms
timestamps = [162] + [162 + 7 * i for i in range(1, 50)]
result = tpot_definitions(timestamps)
for k, v in result.items():
    print(f"{k}: {v}")
print("\nSame run, two 'TPOT' numbers -> always state which definition.")
```

Output:

```
ttft_ms: 162
genai_perf_itl: 7.0
llmperf_tpot: 10.04
```

**The same run, two answers.** GenAI-Perf reports 7.0ms (the true decode gap, excluding the one-time TTFT), while LLMPerf reports 10.04ms (total time / tokens, which amortizes the 162ms TTFT across all tokens, inflating the per-token number). Neither is wrong — they measure different things. But if your dashboard uses one definition and a vendor benchmark uses the other, you will conclude your serving is 40% slower (or faster) than it is, purely from a definitional mismatch. The shorter the output, the bigger the discrepancy, because TTFT is amortized over fewer tokens.

**Why this trap is dangerous.** TPOT/ITL is the metric you optimize for long-generation latency and the one you set SLOs against (Exercise 2's goodput). If you set a 15ms TPOT SLO measured GenAI-Perf-style but your monitoring computes it LLMPerf-style, your alerts fire on a phantom violation — or worse, mask a real one. Comparing your numbers to a published benchmark, a vendor claim, or even last quarter's measurement is meaningless unless you know both used the same definition.

**The reconciliation discipline.** The helper computes *both* definitions explicitly from raw per-token timestamps, so you can: (1) reconcile any tool's number to your own (does their TPOT match my GenAI-Perf or my LLMPerf figure?), (2) state which definition your SLO uses, and (3) store raw timestamps rather than pre-aggregated TPOT so you can recompute under either definition later. This is the metrics-hygiene capstone of the lesson: define TTFT/TPOT/ITL/E2E/goodput precisely (Exercise 1), always report percentiles and goodput not means (Exercise 2), and *always state your measurement definition* so numbers are comparable across tools and time (this exercise). An inference metric without its definition attached is not a measurement — it is a number that happens to look like one.
exercises.starter_code:
def tpot_definitions(token_timestamps_ms):

    # tokens (excludes TTFT). llmperf_tpot = last_timestamp / n_tokens
    # (includes TTFT -> inflated). Return the three (rounded to 2dp where noted).

tests.desc: TTFT is the first token's arrival
the LLMPerf TPOT is inflated by TTFT
